title: Rosemary citrus simmer pot
subtitle: A stovetop simmer pot with fresh rosemary, citrus peel, and cinnamon for a herbal house scent
excerpt: A stovetop simmer pot built from fresh rosemary sprigs, orange and lemon peel, and a cinnamon stick. Set to barely-simmer in a small saucepan; refresh with hot water every hour. Scents an open-plan kitchen and living room for a full afternoon with a savoury herbal note that cuts cooking smells rather than papering over them.
sourceNotes: Simmer pots are a traditional method of scenting a house using kitchen ingredients and a saucepan on the lowest heat. The rosemary and cinnamon give the structural notes; the citrus peel brightens. Watch the water level - simmer pots scorch fast if they run dry.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 small saucepan of simmer water (~500 ml; refreshes for a full afternoon)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Made and used in one session. The ingredients can be combined fresh dry in a jar to keep for a week if you save peels from eating fruit.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Single-use stovetop preparation. Discard the spent botanicals after the session; the water and limp peels can go on the compost. Do not refrigerate and reuse - the warm wet rosemary supports rapid microbial growth.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small saucepan of water set on the lowest heat with aromatic botanicals (citrus peel, herbs, whole spices, dried flowers). The barely-perceptible steam carries the volatile aromatics through a room. Different from boiling potpourri - the goal is steady warm release, not a fast burst of steam. Watch the water level; simmer pots scorch and char fast if they run dry.

Sweet, heavy scents (vanilla, sweet floral, cherry) cover cooking smells by overpowering them - the result is a layered fight. Savoury, fresh, or herbal notes (rosemary, citrus peel, pine, eucalyptus, mint) cut the smell instead by binding to similar volatile compounds. The kitchen reads as clean rather than perfumed.

content.text: is a low-heat saucepan of water with aromatic botanicals - the warm steam carries the volatile aromas through a room. The herbal-and-citrus combination
rather than covering them; it reads as a clean kitchen, not a perfumed one. Suits the afternoon after a long Sunday roast or a fried-fish supper.

Use the smallest saucepan you have - the steam concentration is higher from a small mouth. Lowest heat the hob can hold. Top up with hot water every hour; the pan should never go dry. A simmer pot scorches fast and the burnt-rosemary smell that follows is the opposite of the goal.

items.prepNote: Tap water. Distilled is unnecessary for a simmer pot.
4 fresh rosemary sprigs, 10-15 cm each. Dried rosemary works but the scent is sharper and shorter; fresh is gentler and lasts.
Peel from one lemon - the coloured zest plus a thin layer of pith. Save the flesh for cooking.
One whole cinnamon stick, snapped in two. Adds a warm rounding note under the bright citrus and savoury rosemary.

content.text: Pour the water into the small saucepan. Add the rosemary sprigs, lemon peel, and snapped cinnamon stick. Set the hob to its lowest steady heat. The water should warm to a barely-simmer over 5-10 minutes - small bubbles climbing the side rather than rolling boil. As the steam starts to carry the aromatics, the kitchen will shift in smell within 5 minutes.

Hold on lowest heat. Check every 30-45 minutes. As the water level drops, top up with hot water from the kettle - cold water shocks the rosemary and bruises the scent. The lemon peel will go limp and pale within an hour; the rosemary stays useful for 2-3 hours. Add fresh peel from another lemon for a long afternoon. Switch off the hob when leaving the kitchen unattended for any extended period.

Pour the spent water and limp botanicals into the compost or food waste once the pan has cooled. Do not refrigerate and reuse; the warm-wet rosemary develops microbial growth quickly. Wash the saucepan with washing-up liquid; the rosemary leaves no residue on a steel or copper pan.

Never leave the simmer pot unattended on the hob. The lowest heat setting on a gas burner is hotter than the lowest on an induction or electric ring; the saucepan can go dry in 30-45 minutes if forgotten. Switch off when leaving the room for longer than this. Keep out of reach of children and pets; the steam is hot and the pan handle gets hot.
